# WorldDishAtlas — full content dump

Edited by Lewis Vaughan. Methodology: https://worlddishatlas.com/methodology/
Every entry below carries a verified.source_url and verified.checked_on.

## Cacio e Pepe
URL: https://worlddishatlas.com/dish/cacio-e-pepe/
Cuisine: Italian. Category: pasta. Region: Lazio, Italy.

Roman pasta of pecorino, black pepper, and starchy pasta water. Three ingredients, no fat outside the cheese. The cleanest, hardest Roman dish to make at home.

### History
Cacio e pepe is the older sibling of the Roman pasta family that includes gricia, amatriciana, and carbonara. Roman shepherds carried aged pecorino, dried pasta, and pepper into the mountains because all three travelled well. The friction in modern recipes is the emulsion: how to turn cheese and starchy water into a sauce that coats the pasta without seizing into a stringy mat. The reliable method came out of Roman trattoria kitchens in the 20th century and has since been written about more times than any other three-ingredient dish in Italian cooking.

### Ingredients
- 400g Tonnarelli or spaghetti
- 200g, very finely grated Pecorino Romano
- 2 tsp, fresh-cracked Black peppercorns
- for the pasta water (less than usual; pecorino is salty) Sea salt

### Method
1. Toast the black pepper in a dry wide pan over low heat for 30 seconds until fragrant. Add a ladle of cold water and let it bloom.
2. Cook the pasta in lightly salted water until two minutes short of al dente. Reserve a full mug of pasta water.
3. Transfer the pasta with tongs into the pepper pan. Add a splash of pasta water and toss over low heat for one minute.
4. Take the pan off the heat. Wait 30 seconds for the temperature to drop below 80 C.
5. Add the grated pecorino in three additions, tossing vigorously between each and adding small splashes of pasta water. The cheese should melt into a glossy emulsion, never into clumps.
6. Plate immediately and finish with more pepper. The sauce thickens as it cools, so serve hot bowls.

**Tip.** Grate the pecorino so fine it almost looks like powder. Coarsely grated cheese will not melt evenly and you will end up with the dreaded cheese ball.

Source: https://www.bbcgoodfood.com/recipes/cacio-e-pepe. History: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cacio_e_pepe. Verified: 2026-06-07.

---

## Carbonara
URL: https://worlddishatlas.com/dish/carbonara/
Cuisine: Italian. Category: pasta. Region: Lazio, Italy.

Roman pasta of guanciale, eggs, pecorino romano and black pepper. Four ingredients, no cream, served immediately. The most-copied and most-corrupted dish in Italian cooking.

### History
Carbonara surfaced in Rome around 1944, after American troops arrived with eggs and bacon and Roman cooks turned the rations into a pasta sauce. The first printed recipe ran in the magazine La Cucina Italiana in 1954. The Roman canonical version uses four ingredients: guanciale (cured pork jowl), pecorino romano, egg yolks and black pepper. Cream is not part of any traditional recipe and is the single most-flagged error in modern restaurant versions. The dish takes its name from the Italian word for coal-burner. The most-cited theory connects it to coal-miners eating pasta with bacon and eggs.

### Ingredients
- 400g Spaghetti
- 150g, cut into matchsticks Guanciale
- 100g, finely grated Pecorino Romano
- 4 large, plus 1 whole egg Egg yolks
- to taste, fresh-cracked Black pepper
- for the pasta water Sea salt

### Method
1. Bring a large pot of well-salted water to a rolling boil. Drop the spaghetti.
2. While the pasta cooks, render the guanciale in a wide dry pan over medium heat until the fat is golden and the pieces are crisp at the edges. Do not brown. Cut the heat.
3. In a bowl, whisk the egg yolks, the whole egg, the grated pecorino, and a generous amount of fresh-cracked black pepper into a paste.
4. When the spaghetti is one minute short of al dente, transfer it directly into the guanciale pan with tongs. Add a ladle of pasta water and toss for thirty seconds so the starch coats the strands.
5. Off the heat: pour the egg-cheese mixture over the pasta and toss vigorously. The residual heat cooks the eggs to a glossy sauce. If the sauce tightens, loosen with more pasta water, one tablespoon at a time.
6. Plate immediately. Finish with more pecorino and another crack of pepper.

**Tip.** The single skill is temperature control. The pan must be OFF the heat when the eggs go in. Toss for fifteen seconds before adding pasta water. The residual heat from the pasta and the pan does the cooking.

Source: https://www.lacucinaitaliana.it/ricetta/primi/spaghetti-alla-carbonara/. History: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carbonara. Verified: 2026-06-07.

---

## Pad Thai
URL: https://worlddishatlas.com/dish/pad-thai/
Cuisine: Thai. Category: noodle. Region: Bangkok, Thailand.

Thai stir-fried rice noodles with tamarind, fish sauce, palm sugar, dried shrimp, egg, tofu, peanuts, and lime. National dish, but invented in the 1930s, not centuries old.

### History
Pad Thai is younger than most diners assume. Prime Minister Plaek Phibunsongkhram pushed it as a national dish in the late 1930s as part of a cultural reform programme that also gave Thailand its modern name. Rice noodles were promoted because they used less rice than steamed-rice dishes and reduced Thailand's dependence on rice imports during the war years. The canonical pad Thai pairs sweet (palm sugar), sour (tamarind), salty (fish sauce), umami (dried shrimp) in a balance that distinguishes it from Chinese stir-fries that share the same wok technique.

### Ingredients
- 200g, soaked in cold water 30 min Dried flat rice noodles
- 3 tbsp (dilute concentrate with equal water) Tamarind paste
- 2 tbsp Fish sauce
- 2 tbsp, shaved Palm sugar
- 1 tbsp, soaked and chopped Dried shrimp
- 3 cloves, minced Garlic
- 100g, cubed Firm tofu
- 2 Eggs
- 1 cup Beansprouts
- 1/2 cup, cut into 5cm lengths Garlic chives
- 3 tbsp, crushed Roasted peanuts
- 1, in wedges Lime
- 2 tbsp Neutral oil

### Method
1. Make the sauce: combine tamarind, fish sauce, palm sugar in a small pan over low heat until the sugar dissolves. Reserve.
2. Heat a wok to ripping-hot. Add the oil, then the garlic and dried shrimp; stir for 15 seconds.
3. Add the tofu and stir-fry until edges crisp, about 1 minute.
4. Drain the soaked noodles and toss them in. Pour the sauce over and stir-fry hard, lifting and tossing so noodles do not clump. Cook until noodles soften, about 2 minutes.
5. Push everything to one side of the wok. Crack the eggs into the empty side and let set briefly, then break them up and fold into the noodles.
6. Add the beansprouts and garlic chives. Toss for 30 seconds.
7. Plate. Top with crushed peanuts. Serve with lime wedges, extra fish sauce, dried chili flakes, and sugar on the side so each diner adjusts to taste.

**Tip.** Soak the noodles in cold water for half an hour. Hot soaking softens them too much for the wok. They should still be al dente when they hit the heat.

Source: https://www.bbcgoodfood.com/recipes/pad-thai. History: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pad_thai. Verified: 2026-06-07.

---

## Pho Bo
URL: https://worlddishatlas.com/dish/pho-bo/
Cuisine: Vietnamese. Category: soup. Region: Hanoi, Vietnam.

Vietnamese beef noodle soup. Long-simmered beef-bone broth scented with charred ginger, onion, star anise, cloves, cinnamon. Rice noodles, thin-sliced raw beef, fresh herbs at the table.

### History
Pho emerged in northern Vietnam around the turn of the 20th century, most likely from street carts in the Nam Dinh and Hanoi area. French colonial demand for beef created a beef-bone supply the Vietnamese turned into a noodle soup. The northern (Hanoi) style is clear, restrained, deeply beefy with minimal garnish; the southern (Saigon) style that emerged after the 1954 partition is sweeter, herbier, and served with a side plate of basil, bean sprouts, sawtooth herb, lime, and chiles. Pho bo (beef) is the original; pho ga (chicken) followed in the 1930s.

### Ingredients
- 2kg Beef knuckle and marrow bones
- 500g Oxtail
- 500g (whole piece) Brisket
- 1 large hand, halved Ginger
- 2 large, halved Yellow onions
- 8 pods Star anise
- 6 Whole cloves
- 1 (5cm) Cinnamon stick
- 1 pod Black cardamom
- 1 tsp Coriander seeds
- 3 tbsp Fish sauce
- 1 tbsp Rock sugar
- 400g Fresh banh pho rice noodles
- 300g, frozen 30 min then sliced paper-thin Eye-round beef
- 4, white and green separated, thin-sliced Scallions
- 1 small bunch, chopped Cilantro
- for the table (southern style) Thai basil, beansprouts, sawtooth herb, lime, Thai chiles

### Method
1. Char the ginger and onions over a flame or under the broiler until blackened on the cut sides. This is the flavour foundation.
2. Toast the star anise, cloves, cinnamon, cardamom, and coriander in a dry pan 30 seconds. Tie in cheesecloth.
3. Blanch the bones, oxtail, and brisket in boiling water for 10 minutes. Drain and rinse hard. This step removes scum that would cloud the broth.
4. Return to a clean pot with 6L water, the charred aromatics, and the spice sachet. Bring to a simmer.
5. Cook at the gentlest simmer for 6-8 hours. Skim foam every 30 minutes for the first 2 hours. Hard boil clouds the broth; resist it.
6. Remove the brisket after 90 minutes; refrigerate. Continue simmering the bones for the full time.
7. Strain the broth through cheesecloth. Season with fish sauce and rock sugar.
8. Slice the cooled brisket thinly. Cook the fresh noodles in boiling water 30 seconds; drain into bowls.
9. Top noodles with raw eye-round slices and sliced brisket. Pour the hot broth over (the raw beef cooks on contact). Garnish with scallion and cilantro.
10. Serve with the herb plate on the side for southern style, or restrained for Hanoi style.

**Tip.** Charring the ginger and onion is the single biggest flavour step. Skip it and you have brown water. Char them hard, until the cut faces are properly black.

Source: https://www.recipetineats.com/vietnamese-pho-recipe/. History: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pho. Verified: 2026-06-07.

---

## Pizza Margherita
URL: https://worlddishatlas.com/dish/pizza-margherita/
Cuisine: Italian. Category: pie. Region: Campania, Italy.

Neapolitan pizza of tomato, mozzarella, basil, olive oil. The three colors of the Italian flag, baked at 480 C for 90 seconds in a wood-fired oven.

### History
The marketing story is that Raffaele Esposito of Pizzeria Brandi made this pizza for Queen Margherita of Savoy in 1889, naming it after her and arranging the toppings to match the Italian flag. The first part is documented; the flag claim was added later and is contested. What matters more is that Neapolitan pizza-makers had been pairing tomato and mozzarella on a dough for at least a century before 1889. The Margherita is the modern canonical version of an older Naples tradition. The Associazione Verace Pizza Napoletana codified the rules in 1984: only San Marzano tomatoes, only fior di latte or buffalo mozzarella from Campania, only 60-90 second bakes in a wood oven at 430-480 C.

### Ingredients
- 500g (for 4 pizzas) 00 flour
- 325ml, room temperature Water
- 12g Fine sea salt
- 2g (or 0.5g dried) Fresh yeast
- 200g, crushed by hand, no cooking Whole San Marzano tomatoes
- 200g, torn Fior di latte or buffalo mozzarella
- 8-12 Fresh basil leaves
- 2 tbsp Extra virgin olive oil

### Method
1. Dissolve the yeast in the water. Combine with flour and salt; knead 10 minutes until the dough is smooth and slightly tacky.
2. Cover and let rise at room temperature for 18-24 hours. The long cold fermentation builds the flavour profile.
3. Divide into 4 balls (about 200g each). Rest covered for 4-6 more hours.
4. Preheat a pizza stone or steel in the oven at the maximum temperature for at least 1 hour. A wood-fired oven is the real answer; a home oven at 250 C is the next best.
5. Hand-stretch one ball into a 30cm round with a thicker rim (cornicione). No rolling pin.
6. Top with crushed tomato (spread thin), then mozzarella in scattered pieces, then a small drizzle of olive oil. Basil goes on AFTER baking.
7. Bake 60-90 seconds in a real Neapolitan oven, or 6-8 minutes at home until the rim puffs and the bottom is leopard-spotted.
8. Tear fresh basil over the top. Eat immediately. The Margherita does not wait.

**Tip.** The fermentation does the work. A 24-hour cold rise builds the flavour your oven cannot. Skip it at your peril.

Source: https://www.pizzanapoletana.org/en/pizza-napoletana-stg. History: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pizza_Margherita. Verified: 2026-06-07.

---

## Shoyu Ramen
URL: https://worlddishatlas.com/dish/ramen-shoyu/
Cuisine: Japanese. Category: noodle. Region: Tokyo, Japan.

Tokyo-style ramen with a soy-based tare, chicken-pork dashi, alkaline wheat noodles, chashu pork, menma, scallion, and ajitama soft egg. The original ramen.

### History
Ramen arrived in Japan from China at the end of the 19th century and became Japanese in the 20th. Shoyu (soy sauce) is the original Tokyo profile, formalised in the 1920s and 1930s at the Asakusa Rai-Rai Ken pushcart. Postwar wheat scarcity gave way to American grain shipments in the 1950s and shoyu ramen became a Tokyo street staple. The four building blocks are stable: a wheat noodle made with kansui (alkaline mineral water) that gives ramen its yellow color and bite, a tare (concentrated seasoning) in the bottom of the bowl, a dashi (broth) poured on top, and the toppings. Shoyu tare, chicken-pork dashi, straight medium-thin noodles is the Tokyo profile.

### Ingredients
- 400g Fresh ramen noodles (medium-thin, straight)
- 1kg (for the dashi) Chicken backs and feet
- 500g (neck or trotter) Pork bones
- 20cm piece Kombu kelp
- 20g Dried bonito flakes
- 120ml (for the tare) Japanese soy sauce
- 30ml Mirin
- 30ml Sake
- 200g, sliced Chashu pork (rolled belly, braised)
- 4 halves Ajitama soft-boiled eggs (marinated in shoyu)
- 60g Menma (fermented bamboo shoots)
- 1/2 cup, sliced thin Scallion greens
- 4 squares Nori sheets

### Method
1. Make the dashi. Blanch the chicken bones and pork in boiling water for 5 minutes; drain and rinse. Return to a clean pot with 4L cold water and the kombu. Bring to a slow simmer (never boil hard) for 4-6 hours. Add bonito flakes in the final 15 minutes. Strain through cheesecloth.
2. Make the tare. Combine shoyu, mirin, sake, and 1 tbsp dashi in a saucepan; simmer 5 minutes to soften the alcohol. Let cool. This is the bowl-bottom seasoning.
3. Boil a large pot of water. Cook the noodles 60-90 seconds (check the package; fresh noodles are fast).
4. Warm the serving bowls with hot water; pour out. Put 2 tablespoons of tare in each bowl.
5. Pour 350ml hot dashi over the tare. Stir.
6. Drain noodles thoroughly (excess water dilutes the soup). Transfer to the bowls.
7. Top with chashu, two egg halves, menma, scallion. Stand a nori square on the rim. Eat within 3 minutes.

**Tip.** The bowl must be hotter than you think. A cold bowl drops the dashi temperature in 30 seconds and the ramen experience collapses. Warm with boiling water before assembly.

Source: https://www.justonecookbook.com/shoyu-ramen/. History: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ramen. Verified: 2026-06-07.

---

## Tacos al Pastor
URL: https://worlddishatlas.com/dish/tacos-al-pastor/
Cuisine: Mexican. Category: main. Region: Mexico City, Mexico.

Mexico City pork tacos cooked vertical-spit shawarma-style, marinated in achiote, chiles, and pineapple. Served on small corn tortillas with onion, cilantro, lime, and a slice of charred pineapple.

### History
Tacos al pastor is the Mexican re-invention of Lebanese shawarma. Lebanese migrants settled in Puebla and Mexico City in the early 20th century and brought the vertical spit and marinated meat technique with them. By the 1960s the format was being made with pork (substituted for the original lamb) and a Mexican marinade of dried chiles, achiote, vinegar, and pineapple. The pineapple slice spinning on top of the trompo (spit) is the visual signature of the dish; the juice and acid help break down the pork and a thin sliver gets cut into each taco. Authentic tacos al pastor are made on a vertical gas-fired trompo, sliced thin, and served on hand-pressed corn tortillas the size of a child's hand.

### Ingredients
- 1.5kg, thinly sliced Pork shoulder
- 6, stemmed and seeded Dried guajillo chiles
- 3, stemmed and seeded Dried ancho chiles
- 2 tbsp Achiote paste
- 1/4 cup White vinegar
- 1/2 cup Pineapple juice
- 6 cloves Garlic
- 1 tsp, toasted Cumin seeds
- 1 tsp Dried Mexican oregano
- to taste Salt
- 16 small (taco-size) Corn tortillas
- 1/4 of a fruit, finely diced Fresh pineapple
- 1/2, finely diced White onion
- 1/2 bunch, chopped Cilantro
- 4, in wedges Limes
- to serve Salsa verde or salsa roja

### Method
1. Toast the chiles in a dry pan 30 seconds per side. Cover with hot water and soak 20 minutes.
2. Drain. Blend with achiote, vinegar, pineapple juice, garlic, cumin, oregano, and salt to a smooth paste.
3. Coat the pork slices completely. Marinate refrigerated at least 6 hours, ideally overnight.
4. Without a trompo: lay marinated pork on a sheet pan and broil under high heat 4-5 minutes per side until edges char. Slice thinly.
5. Char the pineapple in a hot dry pan until edges blacken. Dice finely.
6. Warm tortillas on a dry comal for 15 seconds per side.
7. Build each taco: meat, charred pineapple, onion, cilantro. Serve with salsa and lime.

**Tip.** The doubled-up corn tortilla is not a typo. Authentic taco service uses two tortillas per taco so the bottom one absorbs juice and the top one stays intact.

Source: https://www.mexicoinmykitchen.com/tacos-al-pastor-recipe/. History: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tacos_al_pastor. Verified: 2026-06-07.

---
